title: Nepali Chicken Momo with Jhol Achar

ingredients.name: minced chicken (keema)
visual_checks: Chicken thigh mince has better flavour and juiciness than breast mince.
ingredients.notes: Chicken thigh mince has better flavour and juiciness than breast mince.

subs.name: Finely chopped firm tofu or paneer (200 g) with grated vegetables for a vegetarian momo — popular as veg momo across Nepal.

ingredients.name: maida (all-purpose flour), for wrappers
visual_checks: Knead to a firm, smooth dough; the wrapper must be thin but sturdy enough to hold the filling without tearing.
ingredients.notes: Knead to a firm, smooth dough; the wrapper must be thin but sturdy enough to hold the filling without tearing.

subs.name: Gluten-free rice paper wrappers can replace the maida dough for a naturally gluten-free version, though the texture differs.

ingredients.name: black pepper, freshly cracked
visual_checks: Use generously — black pepper is the defining spice in momo filling.
ingredients.notes: Use generously — black pepper is the defining spice in momo filling.

ingredients.name: cooking oil
visual_checks: Added to the filling to keep the mince moist during steaming.
ingredients.notes: Added to the filling to keep the mince moist during steaming.

ingredients.name: tomatoes, for jhol achar
visual_checks: Roasted or dry-fried until charred on the outside for a smoky achar.
ingredients.notes: Roasted or dry-fried until charred on the outside for a smoky achar.

ingredients.name: dried red chillies, for jhol achar
sesame seeds, lightly roasted, for jhol achar
visual_checks: Dry-roast in a pan until golden for best flavour before grinding.
ingredients.notes: Dry-roast in a pan until golden for best flavour before grinding.

subs.name: 2 tbsp roasted peanuts blended into the achar give a similar nuttiness; include peanuts in allergen disclosure.

ingredients.name: water
visual_checks: For kneading (about 100 ml) and for the jhol achar sauce.
ingredients.notes: For kneading (about 100 ml) and for the jhol achar sauce.

steps.text: Knead maida with a pinch of salt and approximately 100 ml water into a firm, smooth dough — it should not be sticky. Cover with a damp cloth and rest for 20 minutes. Meanwhile, combine minced chicken, onion, ginger, garlic, green chilli, coriander, black pepper, oil, and salt in a bowl. Mix well until everything is evenly distributed.

Make the jhol achar: char the tomatoes directly on a gas flame or in a dry frying pan until the skin is blackened. Dry-roast the sesame seeds and cumin seeds in the same pan until golden. Blend the charred tomatoes, roasted sesame and cumin, dried red chillies, a pinch of salt, and 200 ml water into a smooth sauce. Add lemon juice, taste, and adjust salt. Set aside in a bowl.

Divide the rested dough into small balls (about 12–15 g each). On a lightly floured surface, roll each ball into a thin circle about 8–9 cm in diameter — the edges should be thinner than the centre. Place 1 heaped tsp of filling in the centre.

Fold the dough over the filling and pleat the edges together, pinching and twisting to seal firmly. Work from one side, making small overlapping pleats until the momo is completely sealed with a twisted topknot — a tight seal prevents steam from opening the dumpling during cooking.

Lightly oil the steaming basket or idli plates. Arrange the momos so they are not touching each other. Steam over rapidly boiling water for 12–15 minutes until the wrappers turn translucent and are no longer sticky when pressed lightly.
Transfer the hot momos to serving bowls and pour the jhol achar generously over or around them — the momos should sit in a pool of the tangy sauce. Serve immediately while the wrappers are tender.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: keema → Finely chopped firm tofu or paneer (200 g) with grated vegetables for a vegetarian momo — popular as veg momo across Nepal.; maida → Gluten-free rice paper wrappers can replace the maida dough for a naturally gluten-free version, though the texture differs.; sesame-seeds → 2 tbsp roasted peanuts blended into the achar give a similar nuttiness; include peanuts in allergen disclosure.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Steam in a South Indian idli steamer, a multi-tier dhokla steamer, or a pressure cooker with the weight removed and plates on a trivet. The maida wrapper dough is identical to gyoza wrapper dough — very familiar. Sesame seeds and all filling spices are available in every Indian grocery. Jhol achar is similar to a tomato-sesame chutney; if time is short, a blended tomato chutney with roasted cumin is a quick stand-in.
